# Contribution Tiers

This document defines participation levels in dout.dev.

Goal is not rigid hierarchy, but transparent progression from reader to contributor and from contributor to maintainer.

## Principles

- Editorial and technical contributions have separate paths.
- Quality matters more than quantity.
- Trust is built through consistency, respect, and responsibility.
- No role is permanent if Code of Conduct is violated.
- Promotions should be reasoned and visible to community, unless privacy constraints apply.

## Tier 0 - Reader

Reads articles, follows project, shares content.

Can:

- read blog
- share articles
- follow public Discussions

No approval required.

## Tier 1 - Community member

Participates in Discussions or opens relevant issues.

Can:

- comment in article Discussions
- propose ideas
- report errors
- ask questions
- help others

Expectations:

- follow Code of Conduct
- keep discussions relevant and constructive

## Tier 2 - Author candidate

Proposes one or more articles via pull request.

Can:

- open article PRs
- receive editorial review
- update drafts
- discuss their own content

Requirements:

- open article PR
- relevant content
- willingness to accept feedback

## Tier 3 - Published author

Has at least 1 approved and published article.

Can:

- be listed as blog author
- participate in article promotion planning
- propose new articles with stronger process context

Expectations:

- keep content updated when needed
- participate respectfully in Discussions for own articles

## Tier 4 - Contributor

Has at least 3 approved and published articles on dout.dev.

Can:

- be listed as project contributor
- propose editorial series
- suggest improvements to editorial calendar
- help new authors prepare submissions
- join non-private editorial discussions

Requirements:

- at least 3 published articles
- no open Code of Conduct violations
- collaborative behavior in reviews and Discussions

Note: this role comes from editorial contributions. Code PRs are tracked separately for maintainer path.

## Tier 5 - Code contributor

Has at least 1 merged technical PR.

Technical PRs include:

- bug fixes
- refactoring
- tests
- accessibility improvements
- performance improvements
- build, CI, tooling
- site components or features
- technical docs tied to source code

Not technical PRs:

- Markdown articles
- minor editorial fixes
- content-only updates unrelated to source

Can:

- be credited in technical changelogs
- propose architectural improvements
- participate in technical review when invited

## Tier 6 - Maintainer candidate

Has at least 3 approved and merged technical PRs, excluding PRs that only affect Markdown articles.

Minimum requirements:

- at least 3 merged technical PRs
- project knowledge
- consistent Code of Conduct compliance
- clear, non-hostile review behavior
- attention to security, accessibility, and quality
- willingness to handle community workload

Candidacy can be proposed by:

- active maintainer
- the person themselves
- contributor through maintainer request

## Tier 7 - Maintainer

Has review, merge, and moderation responsibilities according to project governance.

Can:

- vote on article PRs
- vote on technical PRs
- manage issues and Discussions
- enforce Code of Conduct
- propose policies
- participate in governance decisions
- publish or coordinate official project content

Responsibilities:

- protect community
- avoid favoritism
- explain rejections and change requests
- declare conflicts of interest
- avoid imposing personal taste through role power
- keep project sustainable

## Tier 8 - Core maintainer

Optional role for sustained responsibility on governance, releases, security, and strategy.

Can:

- coordinate emergency process
- manage access and permissions
- represent project officially
- coordinate editorial calendar
- handle security and moderation escalations

This role should be used only when project growth requires it.

## Role suspension or revocation

A role can be suspended or revoked for:

- Code of Conduct violations
- permission abuse
- hostile or discriminatory behavior
- voting/review manipulation
- long inactivity with still-critical permissions
- undeclared conflicts of interest
- actions that put project or community at risk

Revocation should be reasoned by maintainers and documented when possible.
